reasoning:
The power of the president to adjourn Congress (under Article II, Section 3) has never been used in US history. The base rate for this event is effectively 0%. President Trump raised the possibility in late 2024 and January 2025 as a potential workaround to force controversial Cabinet nominees (like Matt Gaetz, Pete Hegseth, RFK Jr.) through recess appointments if the GOP-led Senate proved recalcitrant. However, as of late February/March 2025, the Senate has rapidly confirmed virtually all of his Cabinet picks (including controversial ones like RFK Jr., Pete Hegseth, Tulsi Gabbard, Lee Zeldin, etc.) with unified or overwhelming Republican support. 

With his primary motive resolved, the crisis that would necessitate the extreme and historically unprecedented measure of adjourning Congress has evaporated. Furthermore, Senate Majority Leader John Thune and other institutionalists in the GOP expressed a strong preference against using the mechanism in order to preserve the Senate's advice and consent prerogatives. Therefore, lacking any new developments or pressing non-cabinet appointments that would trigger an inter-branch confrontation, the probability is at the minimal threshold.
